speaker: interviewer
text: --the 12th of April, 1996. John, could you carry on about your high school education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So after I finished the five year elementary school, my parents decided that they're going to send me to Bratislava, Pressburg, as there was a very good and very well-known Jewish studies school, which they called [NON-ENGLISH]. I mean that's the basis of the Torah. And I think if there was about 800 children in that school. It was a huge sort of a education-- a Jewish education institute. But that was about 200 kilometers away from home. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a boarding school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a boarding school, not in-- a Jewish boarding school in Slovakia. But it wasn't in Bratislava. There was [NON-ENGLISH]. So the better, or more well-to-do Jewish families from our town, they sent their children into that boarding school. We didn't come into that, because we are-- I mean, we could not afford it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the only alternative was to go to-- to-- to Pressburg, to Bratislava. And because I was so-- I was rather little and--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --and young, so my parents sent-- we went together, my-- Alex, my eldest brother, and myself. We went both to Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't get a high school education.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the sister is another story. Sister went to mother's parents to live with her. And there was a gymnasium. And she was attending school there. You see, she did not have to learn Jewish studies so [NON-ENGLISH] these things of-- this-- right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was quite happy about it, because I went with my big brother. And so we rented a room-- or he rented a room. And there was four students. Actually, it's a similar situation-- two brothers, one older, one the younger one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as far as eating, I mean, that was quite customary, you know, out of those circles that because of the-- to-- to provide or to-- or to give facilities to not so rich-- for not so rich children from not so-- such rich parents, to learn. So they've been providing food. That means food that you went, and every day your family-- eat something else in a different place.

speaker: interviewer
text: A family hosted you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A family--

speaker: interviewer
text: Every night of the week.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A different one every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: A different-- not night.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We go for breakfast, for lunch. Because the distance, it wasn't so big. And also for-- for-- I don't think we went for evening. No, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pay for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: The community arranged it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. No, no. The-- the people. I mean, that was a big mitzvah, you know, to sort of support people who are learning the Torah. And that six months or five months which I was with my brother was spent reasonably. I felt safe, protected. And Alex is a wonderful person. He is, and he was. So it was quite a strong and good support in every way. I felt safe. That was from autumn-- so from past Sukkot till Pesach. And then Pesach, after Pesach started, another sort of part of-- of-- studying. So after Pesach, my father took me. And we packed, and he brought me up to Bratislava on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Alex?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Alex? Alex wasn't with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex, no. Alex just really came with me-- I mean, he studied, and he learned. But he came with me, that sort a-- to lead me into a different life and to protect me, to make sure that I'm all right. And I think this was possibly one of the worst part of my life, because I was very much tied to home, to-- mother-- parents, so I felt very good at home and very much loved and very loved. So my father put-- let me in into the place in a room, as a sort of subletting a room. It was gray, dark. The curtains were half-torn, and it looked very depressing. And my father said, you will be all right. Everybody is all right. You will be all right. So he left me. The first thing I did-- I sat down, and I cried for hours. [LAUGHS] Then I said that wouldn't help you So I got up, and I pulled myself together.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the people I-- I was renting the room-- the husband was a teacher in that school. He was not my teacher, but he was a teacher there. And the wife-- they were fairly newly married, so they had no children. And the wife had a green grocery shop somewhere. So they were both so busy, I never seen anybody. I was really on my own. And I did not like to go for breakfast to people somewhere, so I neglected my-- my-- my meals. And I was really going down health-wise. I started--

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you just didn't eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just every-- sometimes here, sometimes within-- and-- I mean, you know, I was used to one type of food. And I can't get used to seven types of food. You know, it's-- it was difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was going emotionally and-- and physically down of-- so I was complaining about tummy aches. So the lady where I lived, she took me-- obviously, a child-- to pediatrician. She tried all sorts of experiments with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just did not improve. So I went back again. So he said, I think I know what's the problem. He needs some more acid to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he prescribed the acid, which I had to take it. You know, I don't touch my teeth. And, obviously, it must have possibly ruined my-- my-- my stomach completely. So one day in the school, I just collapsed. So they took me to a doctor, and they started off what they knew what's wrong with me. I ended up that my father came and took me home. What-- what was the matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the matter with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I mean, you know, I was sick. I mean, there's nobody sort of who could take care of me. I mean, you can't leave a child sort of just to deteriorate completely. And I was really physically sick. I didn't know what's wrong with me. I just had always tummy ache.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a GP by the name of Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]. He was very sort of a-- he was very thorough. So he asked me to come practically daily to see him. And after a while, I mean, he called my father, and he said, Mr. Lowy, I don't think you would believe it, but your son of 11 has got a ulcer duodenum-- a stomach ulcer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, at that time, you know, this was un of heard. So, I mean, I can't prove it 100% what all the symptoms are. So the best thing if you take your child back to Pressburg, but not in school, but into the Jewish hospital, which was a very well-equipped, a very good hospital in Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got all these barium x-rays and everything. And when they discovered that I got ulcer, I mean, it was-- everybody here was astonished. This is impossible. Something must be wrong with someone. So I stayed in the hospital and was cared very well. Well-- and I became quite a celebrity, because all the physicians from Bratislava-- they came, and they wanted to see me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, is it possible? So I was there for a while, and I was taught how to-- how to look after myself and also-- but I definitely-- I couldn't leave home. So I had to be back home. And that was a problem, because there was a very strong gap in my education. I remember I had to go back for the second year high school. I had to go back to Bratislava, because by the way, during-- in the morning, we learned the Jewish studies, and in the afternoon, we learned into the high school, right? So I-- six months I did not have a chance to learn, and I still passed the second year high school. So that was the end of my study, because I came back to Fil'akovo, right? I was already-- I couldn't even go into Lucenec, because I was sick. So I just read and-- everything which I-- is in front of me, and I was sort of teaching myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And mother was very helpful. She, you know-- I had some problem, I always ask her then. So that was my education.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back home, and you became part of the community again, what experiences did you have with the-- the non-Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's amazing to-- to say. I fitted in with the non-Jewish community very well. I mean, from the-- from the-- from the elementary school, I never had any problem. I always was accepted by them. I have quite a lot of friends-- non-Jewish friends. And on the street, they used to sort of ambush Jewish children and stones-- throwing stone. I never had anything. I mean, I can't remember that any of this experience. I always--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no-- you had experienced no anti-Semitism from the non-Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not direct anti-Semitism from them to me personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow-- I don't know what it meant, because people draw to me, and I draw to other people. I sort of-- I looked everyone into their-- you see, the biggest danger is when you are afraid. And I was never afraid. When I saw someone coming, I gave him a nod, rather. How are you-- you know, that sort of a attitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and this way, I-- I never experienced really face to face a sort of anti-Semitism. It happened once. A very good friend of mine-- he's gentile. I used to go and play to his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was Slovakian, or Czech, or something. He was a-- the-- his father was an official at the railways. And the mother called the son in. And I overheard, telling him in Czech, she said, why do you play with that circumcised? So I understood what it meant. I was in shock. I went home, and I went, mother, how is it possible? What's wrong with me? You know, it's-- it's terrible. So mother said, that's the life, darling. I mean, you have to get used to it. Not everybody likes Jewish people. And the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the only practically sort of experience, which I experienced. And it wasn't even really said into my face.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What about the other-- other Jewish people? Were they experiencing anti-Semitism in Fil'akovo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You see, those people-- not so much who are fitted in into the society, right-- payots and this-- you know, when you didn't have-- I mean, people didn't even really know on the street, unless they know that you are Jewish. But the children have said-- the children from the rabbi and shochet and everything-- they-- they did experience. You know, they were sort of-- they laughed at them. They teased them. They spit at them-- any sort of a-- this. But I mean, I-- it didn't happen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now we-- we'll go to-- to you said, 1938-- the beginning of 1939-- who-- were all your-- all the children at home now? Was Alex at home? At the-- at--

speaker: interviewer
text: --it would have been before the war broke out. Who was at home with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I mean, I was away for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Alex was away for a while. And I think Edith-- by then, I think she finished her studies. And then, of course, in 1938-- because Alex was in Nitra, which was Slovakia and then became-- or maybe in Hungary, so there was a-- a border between two countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came home. But not long after he came home, he had to learn some sort of a trade. I think he started to learn the trade already in Nitra where he was. Yes, I think he already finished his apprenticeship.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a glazier and frame maker. So he went to [NON-ENGLISH], which was about 50, 60 kilometers from it. And he got a job in a shop as assistant glazier. So he-- he lived there. And I was at home, and I was still sick. I mean, you know, obviously, ulcer doesn't go away so quickly. And Edith was home. Edith was-- yeah, in that time, Edith was learning to-- to be a couturier. She was travelling to Lucenec every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be a couturier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, designing clothes-- couturier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Designing clothes. And she was very good at it, too. So she-- actually, she finished her study or learning in-- in-- in Lucenec. She was a very clever-- was very good with her hands and sort of-- that was, I think, the right thing to do. And I was-- they tried to send me to-- to learn some trade somewhere, but wherever I went, I felt uncomfortable. And either justifiably or not, I always complained about my stomach. [LAUGHTER] And so when I said my stomach is hurting-- then come home, my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] And Frank was, of course, at home, because he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Frank was-- well, Frank went to school and was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was still a little kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, what happened when the war broke out in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the war came in stages, right? There was a-- I mean, physically, our war really started in 1944. I mean, the rest really-- we just sort of gathered information from radio. The pressure was on-- on us Jews, because the more the Nazis and-- was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spread.

speaker: interviewee
text: --developing and-- and the more the anti-Semitism started-- sort of grew. And we felt it. I mean, either-- either they didn't show it or not, it was in the air. So we felt very, very insecure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you barred from any jobs or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we-- we know all right till about 1940. But I just so really like to remind you something. The biggest shock in our life came in 1940 or '41. I-- I don't remember exactly. As my mother family-- part of the family-- was cut off from us-- was in Slovakia. And the new regime in Slovakia-- I mean, it was a fascist regime. It was [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], and all the thing. I think they made a pact with Germany. I just-- we found out now-- that Germany was buying Jewish stores or Jewish heads [NON-ENGLISH]. And I think 2/3 of the Jewish people in Slovakia were deported. And with them, my mother's two brothers-- married brothers with four children and an unmarried brother-- they were taken away, and we never heard of them. That was, say, in '41. I think the end--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the parents, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: --and her parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her parents, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my parents?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's parents were dead by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the two-- two married brothers and their families and a single brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They are very close. I mean, the rest-- all of them. The whole town, they were cleared and that. So we never heard anything of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear where they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we heard. I mean, they-- they said us. They told us. They, you know, they-- so you get, I mean, to-- there wasn't even a ghetto, because 20 families. I mean, they said that they'd be going away, and they've been told that they will be OK. They will work and, you know, just resettling. And so they took it as a matter resettling, and you know, that God will help, and this will help. They will come back again. You know, it can't last forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as they're being taken away, we never heard of them anymore. We assumed that all of them were taken direct to east Poland or to Ukraine. And they ended up in a mass grave somewhere, which we have no idea where. There is no sign anymore of them. And not only of them, but I think most of the Slovakian Jews. So that was a-- a tragedy. I mean, my mother cried bitterly. I mean, it was unbearable, you know? I mean, you lose one person, is it terrible, but you lose all of them-- you know, it just-- still, for a few months there was some hope. But after you haven't heard of them, so you took it for granted that they've been-- they've been killed. And the contact you had with them was by mail?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one-- yes, always. Mail was going continuously. I mean, mother and them-- there was weekly mail. Every Friday, mother was writing them a letter. And every Saturday, Shabbat, the postman brought us a letter. So there was never contact broken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they left-- after they were left, you didn't have any post from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were-- actually, we had one, which they said that they're going-- you know, they wrote-- they collected the letters. And I think they had-- had a chance to write one letter, which we received. But that was the last sign which we heard of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother didn't keep that letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Might, but I don't know where it is, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I've been changing so many addresses, and we are four children. So, you know, these things easily are misplaced, or maybe they're somewhere. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and now can you describe the years between 1939 and 1944 for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, anyhow, there was a lot of sadness in our place, and we became more and more insecure. So as the regulations worsened all the time, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What regulations were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: --sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: What regulations were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, anti-Jewish regulations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- it was about--

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --so my father-- first of all, the business was-- my father could not work anymore. He had to be safe. He couldn't be employed. My mother had that business. She couldn't manage this business anymore. It was-- had to close down. They took all the licenses, everything away by stages. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father was home now all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was home all the time. There was still some sort of activity for us to go to Budapest. That was a huge city, and it wasn't felt so much anti-Semitism there. And you get lost among the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went up to Budapest, and my uncle went-- the other one. And they took some shoes for selling. They always had to deal somehow something. How far was Budapest from Fil'akovo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 120 kilometers. And so we've been going quite a lot to Budapest. I was-- at that time, I was about 17 years old-- or 16 or 17. I was very anxious to do something, to help something. So I went with father. I went on my own. And at one time, there was-- I was with father. There was some sort of a chemical factory. They were producing a substitute soap, because everything is scarce. You know, at that time, you couldn't get any-- and some cheap cosmetics. So I just you know, went into it, I said, listen. I would like to sell your products.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me a sample of products. I took it back to Fil'akovo. And there was a huge sort of a department store type of thing who were serving the-- the laborers from the-- it actually, it belonged to the-- to the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I get a huge orders. So I was earning money on it. I said that's-- that's a very good idea. But so when they got the goods, there was some substitute soap. And there was nothing but water in something. So they put-- put on the shelves to dry out. And then they ended up nothing. So-- [LAUGHS] --I mean, obviously it was a sort of a fraud or some sort of thing. So I stopped doing them at all. And one thing got clear to us-- all of us-- that with doing nothing in Fil'akovo where we are, and being sort of exposed to people-- they knew exactly that we are Jewish, and so we had to-- the best thing if we move to Budapest. Because we had much more-- much more prospect of-- of-- of doing something, or making some money, or-- or-- or being out of the focus of-- of other people. Because my father was born in Hungary, but while he was in Czechoslovakia it was advantage for him to he become Czechoslavakian--

speaker: interviewer
text: Citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: --citizen-- that's it. So he resigned his Hungarian citizenship and became a Czechoslovakian citizen. And somehow-- I mean, they knew. They found out. They started to pester us, you know? So it's-- I saw the end-- even when--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Fil'akovo, they started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Fil'akovo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Fil'akovo, yes. I mean, it was sort of a-- you are such a Hungarian, and you give up your sort of-- because not every Hungarian Czechoslovakian gave up their Hungarian citizenship. So they'd been working with father and saw a house, an apartment house, going up and for sale. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest. So I practically dragged my father in. So I says, father, let's go and have a look-- maybe just for-- for out of interest what it is. And it was good. And so the owner of that house, who was building, was a Jewish family. And so they already contact her. And I said to father, we've got to come up to live in Budapest. But the rent was extremely high, because it was a modern apartment with every facility, and so forth. So from there on, it never stopped-- Budapest, Budapest, Budapest. We've got to go to Budapest. So finally-- I mean, it wasn't so difficult, I suppose, to agree. We sold the house in Fil'akovo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to find a buyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it has a lot to do with-- with going to Budapest that a-- I mean, the shop was already closed-- that we had an offer--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to buy the house. A fellow came in, and sort of he wanted to buy. He saw this house. Well, it was in a good-- good position.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sold it. And that-- and we rented the place in Budapest. So the whole family went up. There was a little slight accident, you know, which happened, I mean, which was really nearly tragic. You see, to-- to have for Pesach things, like fat and everything, we have to think when the goose season is on, right? I mean, the goose season, don't you make-- you prepare sort of a-- use-- use fat for the everyday life. But for Pesach, it's rare. Because Pesach, you can't get it in our place. And when we've been moving, for somehow, some reason, somebody stole the canister of Pesach fat. Could you imagine my mother's face? I mean, what are we going to do during Pesach? Anyhow, that has been solved, because in Budapest, you could buy anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] --kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Budapest and-- Do you-- can I just ask you? Did your father's brothers remain behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father's brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's brothers bring him and father's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remained in Fil'akovo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. There was another family-- father's sister, which actually they were in Budapest before us, and the husband with the two girls, is still alive. So they survived. They were already in Budapest, so it was easy. It was something which essential for us to move. And secondly, we moved to a big place. And thirdly, we have sister and brother-in-law there, so sort of we just transplanted the family from Fil'akovo-- part of the family in Fil'akovo into Budapest. I mean, financial-wise it wasn't so easy, because we still owed mortgage on the other house. We-- we sent this thing, and all this moving, and all this apartment. So we could not live from the money which we had. We definitely had to earn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father tried to join non-Jewish businessmen, because you couldn't act on your own. I mean, you had to have-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH], you know, who is behind, and there was always a gentile-- to carry on with his-- this other business he was doing, like kitchenware and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the business didn't take off for the simple reason because there was no goods available. There was nothing. You couldn't buy anything-- a shortage in everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942. So we went up to-- no, we went up to Budapest in 1941. And this was in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the pressure came on us very well with all the others-- the relatives and also in the Chinese. My brothers-- we'll come back to this later. Were there-- were there any restrictions on Jews in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same-- everywhere restriction-- but in Budapest, sort of you could go by. It was such a large Jewish city, I would say. And you-- you get lost in the-- in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you see, when some regulation comes, and it's in Fil'akovo, everybody knows. If a regulation comes in Budapest, you know, I mean, nothing-- And what regulations were imposed on you in Budapest? What kind of things were you restricted from doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, anything. You couldn't have your own business. You couldn't study. There were numerous clauses. And you see, in Hungary, really those-- those anti-Jewish regulation goes back till [NON-ENGLISH], which was, you know, still believed in a-- in a democracy in-- in-- in Czechoslovakia. There was already started to have the numerous clauses in-- in Hungary. But to live there and to readjust, it was the last one sort of to be in those-- the destruction of the Jewish people. So we-- we managed there. Really, it wasn't difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, my mother tongue is Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father lived 20 years in Czechoslovakia, and in doing business traveling, he didn't learn a word of Slovakian and/or Czech-- so only Hungarian. And he conducted his business in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's from that--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from that point of view, it wasn't difficult for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So no, we-- actually, it was quite good, because we all spoke very well Hungarian. And also, I never been to a Hungarian school myself. And our accent was not the same as in Budapest. You see, there's a unique Budapest accent, which we never acquired. But we'd been sort of a country accent, which fitted in beautifully, which it came quite handy to me later in my in my-- in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you denied access to cinemas or theaters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everywhere. There was-- life like-- like no war. I mean, you could go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you could go wherever you wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, wherever you want if you had the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't have to wear a yellow star or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not. I mean, you see, there was-- I don't want to really go too much political, because I don't really-- maybe I do not know very well, but there was a ruler at that time, Horthy Miklos, who-- they were allies. Hungary was allies to Germany but wasn't occupied by Germany. So you see, they had to comply with a lot of things, not to be occupied. So there was sort of a cat and mouse game, and-- but the Jews were reasonably safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- so who imposed the restrictions on you? Who imposed all these restrictions on Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? In which is--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, that was the government's line. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were Hungarians who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarians, of course. The government now-- but they didn't have to be forced very much to-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: --so you know, they-- And did they-- did the-- did the people who were imposing the restrictions, whether it was the army or the police-- were they wearing uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean, it was a whole system sort of. And it came through. Like, you know, it-- it came from the Parliament. And the-- the Parliament got direction from-- from-- from Berlin. And then this is affair of-- from Vienna. And-- but it wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just-- can you stop there? We'll go on--
